section.title: Oracles Against the Nations
section.sectionRationale: The book opens with the LORD roaring from Zion and then circling the map: Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and finally Judah. Each oracle follows the same drumbeat, for three transgressions and for four, drawing the listening crowds of Israel to nod along at their neighbors' guilt, until the net has been quietly drawn around the whole region and Israel itself is next.
section.thesis: The God who thunders from Jerusalem holds every nation accountable for its cruelty and covenant-breaking, and His widening circle of judgment is a trap that will close on smug Israel last of all.
section.frameNote: Eight strokes: the roar and the superscription; then judgment on Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and Judah, each announced with the refrain for three transgressions, even four.

units.label: The roar from Zion
units.note: the shepherd of Tekoa hears the lion

verses.text: These are the words of Amos, who was among the sheepherders of Tekoa, what he saw concerning Israel two years before the earthquake, in the days when Uzziah was king of Judah and Jeroboam son of Jehoash was king of Israel.
He said: “The LORD {{shaag|roars}} from Zion and raises His voice from Jerusalem; the pastures of the shepherds mourn, and the summit of Carmel withers.”
This is what the LORD says: “For three {{pesha|transgressions}} of Damascus, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because they threshed Gilead with sledges of iron.
So I will send fire upon the house of Hazael to consume the citadels of Ben-hadad.
I will break down the gates of Damascus; I will cut off the ruler from the Valley of Aven and the one who wields the scepter in Beth-eden. The people of Aram will be exiled to Kir,” says the LORD.
This is what the LORD says: “For three transgressions of Gaza, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because they exiled a whole population, delivering them up to Edom.
So I will send fire upon the walls of Gaza, to consume its citadels.
I will cut off the ruler of Ashdod and the one who wields the scepter in Ashkelon. I will turn My hand against Ekron, and the remnant of the Philistines will perish,” says the Lord GOD.

units.label: Tyre
units.note: they broke a covenant of brotherhood

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “For three transgressions of Tyre, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because they delivered up a whole congregation of exiles to Edom and broke a covenant of brotherhood.
So I will send fire upon the walls of Tyre to consume its citadels.”

units.label: Edom
units.note: he pursued his brother and stifled compassion

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “For three transgressions of Edom, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because he pursued his brother with the sword and stifled all compassion; his anger raged continually, and his fury flamed incessantly.
So I will send fire upon Teman to consume the citadels of Bozrah.”

units.label: Ammon
units.note: they ripped open pregnant women to grab land

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “For three transgressions of the Ammonites, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because they ripped open the pregnant women of Gilead in order to enlarge their territory.
So I will kindle a fire in the walls of Rabbah to consume its citadels amid war cries on the day of battle and a violent wind on the day of tempest.
Their king will go into exile, he and his princes together,” says the LORD.

units.label: Moab
units.note: he burned the bones of Edom's king to lime

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “For three transgressions of Moab, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because he burned to lime the bones of Edom’s king.
So I will send fire against Moab to consume the citadels of Kerioth. Moab will die in tumult, amid war cries and the sound of the ram’s horn.
I will cut off the ruler of Moab and kill all the officials with him,” says the LORD.

units.label: Judah
units.note: they rejected the Law of the LORD

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “For three transgressions of Judah, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because they reject the Law of the LORD and fail to keep His statutes; they are led astray by the lies in which their fathers walked.
So I will send fire upon Judah to consume the citadels of Jerusalem.”

shaag.gloss: To roar, the sound a lion makes over its prey. Amos, a shepherd who knew that sound, opens the whole book with it: the LORD roars from Zion. The word signals both terror and authority; when the lion roars, the prey is already as good as taken, and the land itself mourns at the voice of God.

pesha.gloss: Transgression, rebellion, a willful breach of relationship, stronger than a mere mistake. The refrain for three transgressions, even four is a poetic way of saying the cup is full and overflowing. It frames each nation's cruelty not as isolated crime but as settled rebellion against the Lord of all the earth.

basic.context: Amos was a shepherd from Tekoa in Judah, sent north to wealthy, comfortable Israel around 760 BC. He begins by announcing God's judgment on Israel's neighbors one by one: Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and then Judah. Each is guilty of real atrocities. The crowd would have cheered as their enemies were named, not realizing the circle was closing in on them.
basic.hermeneutics: These are real oracles against real nations for real war crimes: enslavement, atrocity, treaty-breaking. God judges the nations for cruelty they knew was wrong even without His written law. The clever design is that the list moves closer and closer to home, so that self-righteous listeners are set up before the hammer falls on Israel in the next movement.
basic.originalLanguages: The book opens with the LORD roaring, shaag, the lion's roar over its prey. Each nation is charged with pesha, transgression, willful rebellion, and the refrain for three, even four means the guilt has piled up past the limit. The judgment is fire on the citadels, the strongholds people trusted in.
basic.typology: The Judge who roars from Zion and holds every nation to account foreshadows Christ, to whom all judgment has been given. The Lion of Judah who roars here is the same Lion who is also the Lamb, and the day of reckoning He announces points to the final judgment of all peoples.
basic.christ: The God who roars from Zion is the God who will finally judge the whole world through Jesus. The Lion of the tribe of Judah, Revelation 5, is both the roaring Judge and the slain Lamb. The fire that falls on the citadels warns of a judgment that only the cross can shelter us from.
basic.god: God is the Lord of all nations, not a local tribal deity. He sees and remembers every act of cruelty, even among peoples who never had His law, and He will repay. His justice is patient but real; He does not revoke it once the measure of sin is full.
basic.crossReferences: Joel 3:16 also has the LORD roaring from Zion. Romans 2 says the nations are accountable to the law written on their hearts. Revelation 5 shows the Lion of Judah who alone can open the scroll of judgment and blessing.
basic.oneStory: Amos opens the story of God calling the whole world to account. The roar from Zion runs through Scripture to the throne where the Lion who is the Lamb judges every nation, and only those sheltered in Him escape the fire.

context.literary: The movement is built on a relentless, repeating formula: This is what the LORD says, for three transgressions of X, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because they, then the crime, then So I will send fire. The repetition lulls the hearer and builds suspense. Geographically the oracles zig-zag around Israel: northeast, southwest, north, south, drawing an ever-tightening noose.
context.historical: Amos prophesied under Jeroboam II of Israel and Uzziah of Judah, a time of military success and prosperity for the northern kingdom, roughly 760 BC, two years before a memorable earthquake. The crimes named are documented ancient Near Eastern warfare: Aram threshing Gilead, Philistine and Tyrian slave trading, Edom's relentless vendetta, Ammon's atrocities for land, Moab desecrating a king's bones.
context.canonical: Oracles against the nations are a recognized prophetic form, seen also in Isaiah 13-23, Jeremiah 46-51, and Ezekiel 25-32. Amos is unique in using the pattern as a rhetorical trap that folds Judah and then Israel into the same indictment, showing that covenant privilege brings greater, not lesser, accountability.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce that the LORD is Judge of all nations, to expose the self-deception of an Israel that enjoyed condemning others, and to prepare the ground for the shattering word that Israel stands under the same sentence.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The oracles describe God's just judgment on named nations; they do not license human vengeance or nationalism. The abiding truths are God's universal moral government, the reality of guilt for cruelty, and the danger of condemning others while excusing ourselves.

debates.question: Why does Amos include Judah, his own nation, in the list?

views.label: A deliberate rhetorical turn
views.case: Having drawn Israel to applaud judgment on pagan neighbors, Amos names Judah next, the sister kingdom, so that the trap is nearly sprung before the climactic word against Israel; no one is exempt.

views.label: Even-handed covenant justice
views.case: Judah is charged not with atrocity but with rejecting the Law of the LORD, showing that those who have God's word are judged by a higher standard, not a lower one.

debates.bearing: Both readings undercut smugness. God's justice is impartial, and covenant privilege increases responsibility; the closer you stand to God's revealed will, the more accountable you are for it.

study.originalLanguages: The opening verb sets the tone: the LORD shaag, roars, like a lion standing over its kill, a sound the shepherd Amos knew in his bones. Each nation is charged with pesha, willful transgression and rebellion, not accidental fault. The numerical refrain, for three transgressions, even four, is a rising-number idiom meaning the sins have overflowed every limit. And the recurring sentence is fire upon the citadels, the fortified strongholds in which the proud placed their trust; the very symbols of security become fuel for judgment.

typology.type: The LORD roaring from Zion as Judge of nations
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, to whom the Father has given all judgment, Revelation 5:5, John 5:22.

typology.type: Fire consuming the citadels of the proud
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment of every stronghold that exalts itself against God, from which only Christ can shelter the guilty.

study.christ: The roar from Zion is the voice of the God who will judge the world in righteousness through the man He has appointed, Acts 17:31. Jesus is the Lion of the tribe of Judah who alone can open the scroll of destiny, Revelation 5, yet He is also the Lamb who was slain. The same Judge who announces fire on the nations bore the fire of judgment Himself at the cross, so that those who take refuge in Him are not consumed. The tightening circle of Amos 1-2 warns that no nation and no person stands innocent; grace in Christ is the only escape.
study.god: The LORD is sovereign over Damascus and Gaza as truly as over Jerusalem; He is not a tribal god but the moral ruler of every people. He holds nations accountable for cruelty they committed even without His written law, which means conscience and common humanity are real and answerable to Him. His judgment is measured and patient, waiting until transgression is full, yet certain: once He speaks He will not revoke it. And He shows no favoritism, folding His own covenant people into the same court.

crossReferences.note: The LORD roars from Zion and utters His voice from Jerusalem, the same image Amos uses to open the book.
Those who judge others condemn themselves, and the nations are accountable to the law written on their hearts, echoing Amos's trap.
A large collection of oracles against the nations, showing the prophetic form Amos here compresses and sharpens.
The Lion of the tribe of Judah, who alone is worthy to open the scroll, the roaring Judge who is also the Lamb.

study.oneStory: Amos begins the story of the God who calls the entire world to account. The roar from Zion travels across Scripture to the throne of the Lion who is the Lamb, before whom every nation stands. The tightening noose of these oracles exposes that all are guilty, Judah and Israel included, and prepares us to see that the only shelter from the fire is the One who took the fire in our place.

quiz.q: What is the effect of Amos naming Israel's enemies one by one before turning to Israel?

options.text: It draws self-righteous listeners to cheer God's justice, then springs the trap by folding them into the same guilt.
options.note: The widening circle is a deliberate rhetorical trap that closes on Israel.

options.text: It shows God cares about international affairs and war crimes.
options.note: True that God judges the nations, but the design is to expose Israel's own guilt.

options.text: It is simply a geography lesson about the region.
options.note: The oracles are moral indictments, not mere geography.

options.text: It proves God only cares about pagan nations, not His own people.
options.note: The opposite: covenant people are held to a higher standard, as the next oracles show.

quiz.q: Why does God judge nations like Damascus and Ammon who never had His written law?

options.text: Because their cruelty violated a moral order every human conscience knows, and God rules all nations.
options.note: God is Judge of all the earth; atrocity is answerable even apart from Sinai.

options.text: Because they attacked Israel, God's people.
options.note: Some crimes did target Israel, but the charges are for cruelty as such, and Moab's crime was against Edom.

options.text: Because God is a local deity defending His own turf.
options.note: Amos insists the LORD rules every nation, not just Israel.

options.text: Because they worshiped other gods, which is the only sin listed.
options.note: The charges here are atrocity and treaty-breaking, not idolatry.

quiz.q: What does the refrain for three transgressions, even four mean?

options.text: The guilt has piled up past every limit; the cup is full and overflowing.
options.note: The rising-number idiom signals sin that has overflowed all measure.

options.text: Each nation committed exactly four specific sins.
options.note: It is an idiom for fullness, not a literal count.

options.text: God gives three warnings before the fourth strike of judgment.
options.note: It conveys accumulated, overflowing guilt rather than a formal warning system.

options.text: The number has no meaning; it is filler.
options.note: The idiom deliberately signals that transgression has passed the breaking point.

quiz.q: How does the roar from Zion point us to Christ?

options.text: The roaring Judge is the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Jesus, who bore judgment Himself so we can escape the fire.
options.note: The Lion of Judah is both the Judge who roars and the Lamb who was slain.

options.text: It reminds us God is powerful and a bit frightening.
options.note: True, but it specifically anticipates Christ the Judge and refuge.

options.text: It is only about ancient warfare with no link to Jesus.
options.note: Scripture ties the roar and the judgment of nations to Christ.

options.text: It shows Jesus will never judge anyone.
options.note: All judgment has been given to the Son; the roar warns of that day.

quiz.q: Why is Judah's charge, rejecting the Law of the LORD, significant among these oracles?

options.text: It shows that having God's word raises accountability rather than lowering it.
options.note: Covenant privilege increases responsibility, setting up the word against Israel.

options.text: It shows Judah's sin was less serious than the pagan atrocities.
options.note: Rejecting God's revealed word is treated as grave, not trivial.

options.text: It reminds us Judah was Israel's sister kingdom under the same God.
options.note: True, and it presses home that no one, not even the covenant people, is exempt.

options.text: It proves only pagans, never God's people, face judgment.
options.note: Judah and Israel are folded into the very same court.

questions: The crowd loved hearing judgment pronounced on their enemies, until it reached them. Where do you enjoy condemning others while quietly excusing the same thing in yourself?
God held nations accountable for cruelty they knew was wrong even without His written law. What does that say about the promptings of conscience you are tempted to ignore?
The citadels people trusted became fuel for the fire. What are the strongholds you lean on for security that cannot finally save you?
The LORD roars from Zion as Judge of all the earth. How does knowing that God sees and remembers every injustice comfort you, and how does it sober you?
The Lion who roars in judgment is the Lamb who was slain. How does taking refuge in Christ change the way you face the certainty of God's justice?

translationNotes.note: Sheepherders renders a rare Hebrew term that may mean sheep breeders or dealers; Amos was a rural outsider, not a court prophet, which sharpens the confrontation to come.
I will not revoke My judgment is literally I will not turn it back; the same clause recurs in each oracle, underscoring the irreversibility of the sentence once transgression is full.
Where BSB prints an em dash before he and his princes together, it has been rendered as a comma per house style; some read the Hebrew as Milcom, the Ammonite god, going into exile.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Amos 1:1-15 and 2:1-5. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; footnote markers omitted; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
